Post-deploy regression confirmed. Pre-deploy baseline (14:00-14:04) showed healthy metrics: error rates 0.15-0.58%, p99 latency 227-274ms. Immediately after the v681 rolling deploy at 14:05, error rate jumped to 2.23% (above 2% SLO) and p99 to 515ms. By 14:06, degradation worsened: error rate 3.98% (nearly double SLO), p99 858ms (above 800ms SLO). Two consecutive post-deploy samples showing sustained degradation that is escalating, not recovering. Per OC-DEPLOY-02, post-deploy degradation is a regression until proven otherwise and warrants paging rather than waiting it out, as rollback is available. Per OC-BLIP-01, while a single bad sample is not an incident, two consecutive samples showing worsening metrics confirm this is not a transient blip. Both error rate and latency SLOs are being violated on a tier-1 service.

A rolling deploy of v423 completed at 14:06. Pre-deploy metrics (14:00–14:05) were healthy: error rate 0.003–0.006, p99 236–284ms. Starting at 14:06, error rate jumped to 0.02 (at SLO threshold) and p99 to 501ms. Over the next five minutes the degradation worsened and then plateaued: error rate climbed to 0.043 at 14:07 and stabilized around 0.06 (3× the 2% SLO), while p99 latency rose to 1110–1133ms (1.4× the 800ms SLO). This is not a transient — it has been sustained for 5+ minutes with no sign of self-healing. Per OC-DEPLOY-02, degradation beginning shortly after a deploy is a regression until proven otherwise, and there is a rollback available, so this warrants a page rather than waiting it out.

Per runbook OC-DEPLOY-02, degradation beginning shortly after a deploy is a regression until proven otherwise, will not self-heal, and has a rollback available — page rather than wait it out. This is not a transient (OC-BLIP-01) as it is sustained across multiple samples and directly correlates with the deploy.
